State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5901          322-6441            322-6444


August 16, 1982


Walter V. Hays
Town Attorney
Town of Los Gatos
P.O. Box 949
Los Gatos, CA  95030

     Re:  Brent Ventura; Follow-up on Advice
          Letter No. A-82-083
          Our No. A-82-143

Dear Mr. Hays:

     Thank you for your recent letter following up on a series
of advice letters regarding City Councilman Brent Ventura.
Reference is made to our letters, A-82-083, A-82-063 and
A-80-090, each of which is incorporated herein by reference.

     By way of recapitulation, Mr. Ventura owns an interest in
real property in excess of $1,000. It is in the form of part
ownership of a seven unit apartment complex in the Town of
Los Gatos.  The rent control ordinance extension decision
which is pending will foreseeably have some effect upon the
rental income derived from Mr. Ventura's real property
interest.  The question is one of whether the foreseeable
effect will be material. In the previous letters,
materiality was present as to some decisions and not as to
some others.

     Mr. Ventura now requests our advice with respect to
changes in the facts that our previous letters were based
upon.  The monthly rents from the seven apartments previously
totaled $1,400-1,600/month.  Based upon that fact, a material
financial effect would be $70-80/month, total, or roughly $10
to $13 per unit per month.  Now, Mr. Ventura reports that the
rent has risen to $1,788 total per month.  A 5% increase
would then be $90 per month, or roughly $15 per unit per
month.  Therefore, if the difference of having or not having
the rent control ordinance will change Mr. Ventura's rents on

Walter V. Hays
August 16, 1982
Page 2


his apartments by less than $15 per unit per month, he would
have no conflict of interest. If it is reasonably fore-
seeable that the difference will exceed $15 per unit per
month, then he must disqualify himself. If we assume a
concomitant increase in the value of the property (from
$400,000 to $450,000), then the effect on its value by virtue
of the ordinance continuing or expiring would have to be less
than $2,250; otherwise the effect of the decision would be
material and disqualification would be required.

I trust that this letter satisfactorily responds to
Mr. Ventura's question regarding the changed circumstances.
As always, it is left up to you and he, who are familiar with
the facts locally, to determine what the reasonably fore-
seeable effect of the decision will be on Mr. Ventura's
interest in the apartment complex. If you have any
questions, I may be reached at (916) 322-5901.

                    Sincerely,

                    [signature]
                    Robert E. Leidigh
                    Counsel
                    Legal Division

REL:plh

TOWN of LOS GATOS
Town Attorney
354-6880

July 21, 1982

Robert E. Leidigh, Esq.
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re:    Brent Ventura; Follow-Up of Advice Letter 82-083

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

In September, 1982, the Town Council will be considering a temporary, three-month extension of the ordinance identified in our earlier correspondence, and Mr. Ventura has again asked if he must disqualify himself. On July 20, 1982, in response to your letter of May 13, 1982, Mr. Ventura advised me that the gross rents on the apartment are now $1,788/month as compared to $1,400-$1,600/month at the time of your July 17, 1980 letter.

I have given Mr. Ventura my own opinion on the subject, a copy of which is enclosed. Because he is under strong pressure to vote, however, he has also requested that I obtain a brief formal opinion from you.

Thank you again for your assistance on this matter.

Sincerely yours,

Walter V. Hays
Town Attorney

WH:ms

cc:  Brent Ventura

CIVIC CENTER  •  110 EAST MAIN STREET  •  P.O. BOX 949  •  LOS GATOS, CALIFORNIA 95030

TOWN of LOS GATOS
Town Attorney
354-6880

July 21, 1982


To:     Brent Ventura

From:   Walt Hays

Re:     Vote on Rent Mediation Ordinance

Issue

Given the fact that the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC) has already
ruled that your interest in a family trust disqualified you from voting for
adoption of the rent mediation ordinance when it was first proposed, would you
also be disqualified from voting for (1) permanent adoption, or (2) a brief
extension?

Summary Answer

You are disqualified to vote on either permanent adoption or a brief extension
of the ordinance, because a vote not to extend the ordinance would cause it
to lapse, and it is reasonably foreseeable that such a decision would have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, or your property interests.

In his letter to me of May 13, 1982, Mr. Leidigh of the FPPC suggested that if
the value of your property interest had increased sufficiently, it might be
possible that your vote on extension of the ordinance would not have a material
effect on it. In my opinion, however, the revised rent figures you submitted to
me on July 20, 1982, do not indicate a sufficient increase to enable Mr. Leidigh's
exception to apply.

Per your request, I have asked Mr. Leidigh to give us his formal opinion on the
issue.


                                            Walter V. Hays
WH:ms
cc:  Mr. Robert E. Leidigh ✓

         CIVIC CENTER • 110 EAST MAIN STREET • P.O. BOX 949 • LOS GATOS, CALIFORNIA 95030

sections.conclusion: Therefore, if the difference of having or not having the rent control ordinance will change Mr. Ventura's rents on his apartments by less than $15 per unit per month, he would have no conflict of interest. If it is reasonably foreseeable that the difference will exceed $15 per unit per month, then he must disqualify himself. sections.question_synthetic: Given that Councilman Brent Ventura's rental income from an apartment complex has increased from $1,400-1,600/month to $1,788/month, does he have a material financial conflict of interest requiring disqualification from voting on a rent control ordinance extension?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Ventura has a conflict of interest requiring disqualification if the ordinance's effect on his rents exceeds $15 per unit per month or on property value exceeds $2,250, based on updated financial figures. The FPPC establishes quantitative thresholds for materiality based on the changed financial circumstances.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains FPPC advice letter (A-82-143) dated August 16, 1982, responding to a request from Town Attorney Walter Hays regarding updated facts. Question and conclusion sections clearly identified. Also includes supporting correspondence from July 21, 1982 (incoming request from Hays and town attorney opinion to Ventura). Main FPPC response is well-structured with quantitative guidance.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel responds to a follow-up inquiry regarding Councilman Brent Ventura's potential conflict of interest in voting on a rent control ordinance extension, establishing specific materiality thresholds based on updated rental income figures and property valuation.
